summary: The session focused on the working conditions at the Ford Dagenham plant in the early 20th century, highlighting the harsh environment and the subsequent improvements brought about by trade unions and worker strikes. The user expressed appreciation for the progress made in workers' rights and workplace safety over the years.
facts: The Ford Dagenham plant in the early 20th century had harsh working conditions, with workers expected to work 10-12 hour shifts, six days a week, in a hot and noisy environment with minimal safety measures. The Transport and General Workers' Union (TGWU) began organizing workers at the Dagenham plant in the 1930s, leading to improvements in pay and working conditions. In the 1940s and 1950s, the Ford Dagenham plant introduced safety measures such as protective gear and fire alarms. Workers at the Ford Dagenham plant went on strike in the 1960s to demand better pay and working conditions, contributing to the introduction of the Equal Pay Act of 1970. By the 1970s, working conditions at the Ford Dagenham plant had significantly improved, with better pay, a more reasonable work-life balance, and safer environments.
